summary: I was born and raised in Tacoma, Washington. My family lived there before the war, and it was where my father ran a small produce market before the Great Depression hit. We lived near the Puget Sound, which is where my grandfather went fishing the day Pearl Harbor was attacked. Tacoma was also the place where the mayor opposed the evacuation of Japanese Americans, which was a rare stance at the time.

Our first stop after evacuation was the Pinedale Assembly Center in Fresno County, California. We stayed there for about three months before being sent to Tule Lake. It was a temporary place where many Japanese Americans were gathered before being relocated to more permanent camps.

Pinedale Assembly Center was located in Fresno County, California, where my family was held for a few months in 1942. It was the first place we were sent after leaving Washington. Fresno County was just the starting point before we were moved to Tule Lake.

After Pinedale, we were sent to Tule Lake War Relocation Center, which was a huge camp and felt like a dust bowl. I was a teenager then, so I had friends and went to school there. It was at Tule Lake that the loyalty oath was administered, and my father decided to affirm his loyalty to the U.S. so we could stay. I also got my first job there by using a relative’s name to work as a waitress in the mess hall.

Many people from Washington preferred to go to Minidoka War Relocation Center, but because our family signed up as a group, we were sent elsewhere. I never actually lived in Minidoka, but I remember it was a common choice among those from my home state.

Our family was sent to Jerome War Relocation Center in Arkansas after Tule Lake. Jerome was a swampy area and not a very comfortable place. I was in my junior year of high school while we were there. After about a year, the camp was closed, and we were moved again.

After Jerome closed, we were sent to Heart Mountain Relocation Center in Wyoming, near Yellowstone National Park. I spent my senior year of high school there and even got to visit Yellowstone on a $5 chartered bus trip. Near the end of our time there, the camp was more relaxed, and we could walk out to the nearby Shoshone River for social gatherings like weenie bakes.

While at Heart Mountain, I had the chance to visit Yellowstone National Park. For just five dollars, a bus was chartered so we could get out and enjoy the park. It was a rare and memorable experience during our confinement in the camp.

Near Heart Mountain camp, there was the Shoshone River where we used to go for weenie bakes. We would sometimes walk out of the camp and be invited by local Boys Clubs or other groups for social events. It was one of the few ways we could enjoy some freedom and normalcy during that time.

Growing up during the Great Depression was tough for my family. My father had a small produce market but had to file for bankruptcy in the early 1930s. He took menial jobs like farm work and eventually became a foreman shucking oysters, which is how I learned to love oysters. We lived in poverty, with no refrigerator or telephone until my parents started a laundry business and got out of debt.

I remember the day Pearl Harbor was attacked; it was a Sunday. I was coming home from Buddhist Sunday school when I heard men talking about it. My mother found out from friends, but my father and grandfather had gone fishing near Puget Sound. When my grandfather returned, the FBI was waiting and took him away that very night because he was a community leader.

We lived near Puget Sound in Tacoma, Washington. My father and grandfather went fishing there on the day Pearl Harbor was attacked. It was a familiar place for us and part of our daily life before the war changed everything.

The FBI came to our home the night Pearl Harbor was attacked and took my grandfather away because he was a civic leader involved in church and Japanese language school. We didn’t know where he was taken at first, but later found out he was sent to camps in Missoula, Montana, and then Santa Fe and Lordsburg, New Mexico. It was a frightening and confusing time for our family.

My grandfather was taken by the FBI to a camp in Missoula, Montana after Pearl Harbor. I later heard from an Italian man there that they had a mock hearing. It was a difficult experience for him, and he was later transferred to other camps in New Mexico before being released after about a year.

After Missoula, my grandfather was sent to a camp in Santa Fe, New Mexico. It was part of the series of FBI camps where he was held during the war. I wrote to him weekly in broken Japanese while he was there, hoping to keep in touch despite the hardship.

Lordsburg, New Mexico was another camp where my grandfather was held after being moved from Missoula and Santa Fe. These FBI camps were very different from the War Relocation Centers where our family was sent. I wish I had been older to understand his experience better.

My mother was born in Napa, California in 1907. When she was about seven, she was sent to Japan to be raised by her paternal grandparents for ten years. She returned to the U.S. as a teenager but never really connected with her mother, making her childhood quite lonely.

Japan was where my mother spent ten years of her childhood after being sent there at age seven. Our family on her side came from samurai stock, and we have records going back over 600 years. My grandfather on my mother’s side was from a seaside town and part of the Mori clan. My father decided not to return to Japan after the loyalty oath, wanting to raise his children in the U.S.

My mother’s family belonged to the Mori clan, a samurai lineage from a seaside town in Japan. We have family records written in Japanese that go back over 600 years, including commendations for battles fought well. Although I can speak Japanese, I can’t read the old documents, but I treasure the connection to my heritage.

During my time at Tule Lake, I wanted to buy things from the Montgomery Ward catalog. To earn spending money, I got a waitress job in the mess hall using a relative’s name since I was too young. I earned $16 a month, which was a lot for me and helped me buy personal items like new shoes.

Fresh tuna was brought into the camp mess halls from places like Denver. My mother occasionally worked in the canteen and would bring food home from the mess hall. It was one of the ways families managed to supplement their meals in camp.

After the war, my father returned to Oakland and took up gardening as a hobby and later as work. I also lived in Oakland after the camps, and it was where I reconnected with an old boyfriend from camp during reunions. Oakland became home again after the long years of displacement.

My boyfriend from camp was living in San Jose after the war. We dated again after I moved to Oakland, although he eventually married someone else. San Jose was part of the area where many of us resettled after the camps closed.

My boyfriend’s girlfriend left for Nebraska during the war, which is why he and I started dating in camp. She later returned, and he married her. Nebraska was one of the places some Japanese Americans were sent or moved to during the war years.

In recent years, I’ve traveled extensively with my boyfriend and children, including going through the Panama Canal. Traveling has been a big part of my life after the war, something I couldn’t have imagined during those difficult years in camp.
The transcript does not mention Toshi Mizoguchi, so I have no personal story or connection to share about this person.
Dorothea Lange is not mentioned in my story, so I don’t have any memories or experiences related to her.

The mayor of Tacoma who opposed the evacuation later became a U.S. senator. He was one of the very few public officials who stood against the forced removal of Japanese Americans. His opposition was a rare and important act of courage during that time.
